claims.flip_evidence_text: provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination to constitute, and none of the following shall be taken into account in determining whether there is, or would reasonably likely to be, a Material Adverse Effect on the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole: (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally ... provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Material Adverse Effect” means with respect to the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, any change, event, occurrence, circumstance, condition or effect (each, an “Effect”) that, individually or taken together with all other Effects, and regardless of whether or not such Effect, considered together with all other Effects, would constitute a breach of the representations or warranties made by such Person in this Agreement (i) would, or would reasonably be expected to, be or become materially adverse to the financial condition (including assets and liabilities, taken as a whole), business, operations or results of operations of the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole; provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination to constitute, and none of the following shall be taken into account in determining whether there is, or would reasonably likely to be, a Material Adverse Effect on the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole: (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally, (B) changes generally affecting the industry in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate, (C) changes in Applicable Legal Requirements, (D) changes in GAAP, or other accounting regulations or principles or interpretations thereof, that apply to the Company and the Subsidiaries, (E) national or international political conditions, any outbreak or escalation of hostilities, insurrection or war, or acts of terrorism, (F) epidemics, quarantine restrictions, wildfires, earthquakes, hurricanes, tornadoes, other natural disasters or similar calamity or crisis, (G) changes in the trading volume or trading prices of such entity’s capital stock in and of themselves (provided that such exception shall not apply to any underlying Effect that may have caused such change in the trading prices or volumes), (H) any failure, in and of itself, to meet market revenue or earnings expectations, including revenue or earnings projections or predictions made by the Company (whether or not publicly announced) or securities or financial analysts and any resulting analyst downgrades of the Company’s securities in and of themselves (provided that such exception shall not apply to any underlying Effect that may have caused such failure or such downgrades), (I) changes in the Company’s and the Subsidiaries’ relationships with employees, customers, distributors, suppliers, vendors, licensors or other business partners as a result of the announcement or pendency of the Original Agreement, this Agreement or the anticipated consummation of the Merger and the other Transactions (provided that the exceptions in this clause (I) will not apply with respect to the representations 5 and warranties contained in Section 2.3(b) or, solely to the extent related to the representations and warranties contained in Section 2.3(b), Section 6.3(a) and Section 7.1(f)), (J) any actions taken or failure to take action, in each case, that Parent has expressly in writing approved, consented to or requested and (K) any actual or threatened Stockholder Litigation; provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate, or (ii) would, or would reasonably be expected to, prohibit the Company’s ability to consummate the Transactions in accordance with this Agreement and Applicable Legal Requirements in the United States (provided that the absence of or failure to obtain any consent, approval, waiver or clearance from any Governmental Entity under Antitrust Laws with respect to the Transactions shall not constitute a Material Adverse Effect under this clause (ii)). (Pages 10-11)

Question:
General economic and financial conditions: subject to "disproportionate impact" modifier

Llama answer:
General economic and financial conditions, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally, are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless they disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate.

Canonical claims:
- claim-65776cb35537: General economic and financial conditions, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally, are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless they disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to evaluate a claim against a contract excerpt, a question, and a model answer (Llama answer).

 The task is to return for each claim:
 - correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
 - load_bearing_label: yes or no
 - flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
 - notes: optional short note

 Contract excerpt defines "Material Adverse Effect" and lists exceptions (A) through (K). Specifically:
 - (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally.
 - There is a "disproportionate impact" carve-out: "provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate"

 The question is: "General economic and financial conditions: subject to 'disproportionate impact' modifier"

 The Llama answer is: "General economic and financial conditions, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally, are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless they disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate."

 Canonical claim:
 - claim-65776cb35537: General economic and financial conditions, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally, are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless they disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate.

 I need to evaluate if this claim is correct based on the contract.

 The claim says these conditions are "not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless" disproportionate. This seems accurate based on the excerpt. The excerpt says "none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves... to constitute... a Material Adverse Effect" and lists (A) general economic conditions etc. Then the proviso says "the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect..." So yes, general economic conditions are excluded unless disproportionate impact.

 So correctness_label: true.

 Now load_bearing_label. This is a bit trickier. What does "load-bearing" mean in this context? Usually in these annotation tasks, a "load-bearing" claim is one where if you flip its truth value (i.e., if it were false), it would change the overall answer/support in a meaningful way. Or it might refer to whether the claim is central to the answer. Let me think about the specific instructions. The user says "load_bearing_label: yes or no". In many legal annotation tasks, a claim is "load-bearing" if it is actually used to support the conclusion or answer. But since the prompt doesn't define it explicitly, I need to infer from standard usage in this research pilot.

 Wait, the user provided the canonical claim which exactly matches the Llama answer. The question asks about general economic and financial conditions subject to disproportionate impact modifier. The claim is essentially the answer.

 But what is "load-bearing"? Let me think. In some prior work (like Atticus or similar datasets), a claim is load-bearing if it's a key assertion that needs to be checked. If the claim is false, does it undermine the answer? Or is it just a restatement? Actually, looking at the structure, the canonical claim is identical to the Llama answer. The question is asking specifically about this topic. The claim captures the essence of the answer.

 However, "load-bearing" might mean: does the claim bear the weight of the answer? Is it the main point? If the claim is false, would the answer be wrong? Yes, because the claim *is* the answer. But wait, the Llama answer *is* the claim. So is the claim load-bearing? It seems yes, because it's the direct response to the question.

 But I need to be careful. In some annotation schemes, if a claim is simply a direct paraphrase of a specific clause and is the entire answer, it's load-bearing. The "flip_evidence_text" would be the text that would contradict it if flipped. If load_bearing_label=yes, I need to provide flip_evidence_text.

 What would flip the claim? The claim states that general economic/financial conditions are NOT considered MAE UNLESS disproportionate. To flip this, we'd need evidence that they ARE considered MAE even without disproportionate impact, or that the disproportionate impact clause doesn't apply. The contract text that would flip it would be something showing that clause (A) exceptions apply unconditionally or something. But the actual text supports it.

 Wait, "flip_evidence_text" might mean: what text in the contract would serve as evidence if the claim were false (i.e., flipped)? Or is it the text that supports the flip? The instruction says: "flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string". This suggests that if the claim is load-bearing, we need to provide the evidence text that would flip the claim's truth value. But since the claim is true, the flip evidence would be text that contradicts it. However, the contract doesn't have text that contradicts it. Hmm.

 Alternatively, maybe "flip_evidence_text" means the text that supports the claim's truth value, and if you flipped it, it would make it false? No, that doesn't make sense.

 Let me reconsider. In some annotation frameworks (e.g., from the paper "Evaluating Legal Claim Verification" or similar), "load-bearing" means that the claim is entailed by the text and is central. "Flip evidence" might be the specific span that, if interpreted differently, would flip the claim. Or perhaps it's the span that supports the claim, and the idea is that if that span were absent or different, the claim would flip.

 Actually, I recall a specific dataset or task: the "Atticus" dataset or "MAUD" dataset might have a "load-bearing" annotation. But I'm not sure.

 Let me think about the logic. If load_bearing=yes, the claim is pivotal. The flip_evidence_text would be the text that determines whether the claim is true or false. In other words, it's the evidence that bears the load. If you flip the meaning of that evidence, the claim flips. So I should provide the text span that contains the critical condition.

 In the contract, the critical text is: "provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves... to constitute... a Material Adverse Effect... (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial... markets... ; provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect..."

 So the flip_evidence_text would be something like: "provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination to constitute... (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof... provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate"

 But wait, the user says "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." So I should extract the flip evidence from the contract excerpt.

 Let's re-read the claim: "General economic and financial conditions, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally, are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless they disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate."

 This is true. The load-bearing nature: since the claim directly answers the question and is supported by the text, it's load-bearing (yes). The flip evidence would be the text that establishes the exception and the disproportionate impact carve-out. If that text were absent or opposite, the claim would flip.

 So I'll mark:
 - correctness_label: true
 - load_bearing_label: yes
 - flip_evidence_text: the relevant portion of the contract excerpt that contains the exception and the disproportionate impact clause. I need to quote it exactly or closely.
 - notes: empty or brief.

 Let me check if there is any subtlety making it partially_true. The claim says "general economic and financial conditions". The contract says "changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets". The claim uses "general economic and financial conditions, including any disruption thereof" which is a slight condensation but accurate. It says "are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless...". The contract says they are excluded from being deemed MAE in and of themselves, and the exceptions don't apply if disproportionate. This is essentially equivalent. So true.

 Is it load-bearing? The question is specifically about this. The claim is the answer. So yes.

 Flip evidence text: I should include the specific clauses. Let me select the text from the excerpt:

 "provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination to constitute, and none of the following shall be taken into account in determining whether there is, or would reasonably likely to be, a Material Adverse Effect on the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole: (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally... provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate"

 I need to format it as a JSON or similar? The user didn't specify a strict format, just said "For each claim return: ...". Probably a list of fields.

 I'll provide:

 correctness_label: true
 load_bearing_label: yes
 flip_evidence_text: "provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination to constitute ... (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof ... provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate"
 notes: ""

 Wait, I should double-check if the claim is exactly load-bearing. In some contexts, if a claim is just a restatement of a single clause without inference, it might be considered not load-bearing? But that seems unlikely. Load-bearing usually means "does this claim carry weight in the argument?" Since the answer consists entirely of this claim, it is definitely load-bearing.

 Let me think if there's any reason it would be partially_true. The Llama answer says "General economic and financial conditions... are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless...". The contract says "none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves... to constitute... a Material Adverse Effect". It doesn't say they are not considered MAE under any circumstances; it says they are not deemed to constitute MAE in and of themselves. The carve-out says the exceptions don't apply if disproportionate. So the net effect is: if disproportionate, they CAN be deemed MAE. The claim says "are not considered a Material Adverse Effect unless [disproportionate]". This is a reasonable summary. Is it fully true? The contract says "none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves... to constitute...". The claim uses "are not considered". This is accurate in the context of the overall definition because if they are disproportionate, they are taken into account and could contribute to MAE. The claim's "unless" structure captures the exception to the exception. So true.

 One more check: The claim says "the regions in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate" while the contract says "the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate". The Llama answer dropped "in the world". The claim is based on the Llama answer. The canonical claim matches the Llama answer. The question is whether this omission matters. The contract says "regions in the world". Dropping "in the world" is a minor paraphrase that doesn't change meaning materially. The claim is still true.

 I'll go with true and load-bearing yes.

 For the flip_evidence_text, I should provide the exact text from the contract excerpt that would flip the claim if it said the opposite. The key text is the proviso and clause (A). I'll quote the relevant part.

 Let me construct the output clearly.

 Actually, looking at the instruction, it might want a specific format like YAML or bullet points. I'll use a clear structured format.

 Since there is only one claim, I'll return the fields for claim-65776cb35537.

 Final check: Is there any subtlety about "financial conditions" vs "financial markets"? The contract lists "financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets". The claim says "financial conditions". The contract says "general economic conditions or financial ... markets". The claim merges them as "general economic and financial conditions". This is a slight conflation. The contract has "general economic conditions" and then separate financial markets. But the claim says "general economic and financial conditions, including any disruption thereof". The phrase "including any disruption thereof" comes from clause (A) which applies to the markets. The claim is slightly imprecise but still essentially true as a summary. I think "true" is correct.

 Wait, the question says: "General economic and financial conditions: subject to 'disproportionate impact' modifier". The claim is directly answering that. It's true.

 Okay, I'm confident.

 Output:

   correctness_label: true
   load_bearing_label: yes
   flip_evidence_text: "provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination to constitute, and none of the following shall be taken into account in determining whether there is, or would reasonably likely to be, a Material Adverse Effect on the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole: (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally ... provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate"
   notes: ""

 I'll make sure the flip_evidence_text is a single string without line breaks if possible, but it's okay.

 Actually, maybe I should keep it concise: just the two provisos and clause (A). I'll include enough to be clear.

 Let me refine the flip_evidence_text to be exact from the excerpt:

 "provided that none of the following shall be deemed in and of themselves, either alone or in combination to constitute, and none of the following shall be taken into account in determining whether there is, or would reasonably likely to be, a Material Adverse Effect on the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole: (A) changes in general economic conditions or financial, credit, foreign exchange, securities, currency, capital or other financial markets, including any disruption thereof, in the United States, any other country or region in the world or the global economy generally ... provided that the exceptions in clauses (A) through (F) shall not apply to the extent that such changes disproportionately and adversely affect the Company and the Subsidiaries, taken as a whole, as compared to other participants in the industry and the regions in the world in which the Company and the Subsidiaries operate"

 This seems good.
